coredata.dc:title: A specific short peptide-assisted enhanced chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CRET) for label-free and ratiometric detection of copper ions in complex samples
coredata.dc:creator: Xiong, Yamin
Zhou, Lihong
Peng, Xiaoxia
Li, Huiling
Wang, Hui
He, Leiliang
Huang, Peili
coredata.dc:description: Convenient and rapid detection of Cu2+ in serum has important clinical significance for related diseases. Herein, a ratiometric detection of Cu2+ based on specific short peptide (SPGH)-assisted enhanced chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CRET) has been constructed. An interesting phenomenon that tetra-peptide with the sequence of Ser-Pro-Gly-His (SPGH) was able to specifically and remarkably enhance the catalytic performance of Cu2+ has been discovered, the enhancement mechanism is mainly due to the significant increasing generation of Cu2+-induced hydroxyl radical after addition of SPGH. The chemiluminescence (CL) of the luminol-H2O2 system was efficiently catalyzed by the formed Cu(SPGH)2 complex, which acts as an internal light source to triggers on the fluorescence of QDs via CRET, allowing the ratiometric detection of Cu2+ within 15 min with improved selectivity and sensitivity. The relative standard deviation was lower than 6.38 % due to the ratiometric signal improving its anti-interference ability. Importantly, it is feasible to detect total Cu in human serum, and the results were consistent with that of ICP-AES (r = 0.9321, n=55). The reliable, rapid, yet simple ratiometric detection of Cu2+ in serum based on SPGH-assisted enhanced CRET provides a promising potential alternative method for Cu2+ detection in clinical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Peptide
Chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer
Ratiometric detection
Copper ions
Serum

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Thermodynamic parameters of the interaction between Cu2+ and SPGH
The FTIR spectra of Cu2+ coordinated with SPGH
Mechanism of SPGH-assisted enhanced the catalytic ability of Cu2+

Peptide-assisted enhanced CRET for determination of Cu2
+
Detection of Cu2+ in complex samples
Thermodynamic parameters of the interaction between Cu2+ and SPGH
The FTIR spectra of SPGH and Cu(SPGH)2
Explore the mechanism of SPGH-assisted enhanced catalytic capacity of Cu2+
The procedure and feasibility of peptide enhanced CRET
Determination of Cu2+ in complex samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The influence of peptides with different sequences on the catalytic ability of Cu2+; (B) Comparison of the CL enhanced by different ligands; (C) Enhancement ability of SPGH and EDTA to the catalytic ability of metal ions. (The concentrations of ligands and Cu2+ were 10 mM and 10 μM, respectively.).

(A) The thermogram of Cu2+ binding to SPGH and nonlinear regression of heat vs mole ratio of Cu2+ to SPGH; (B) The FTIR spectra of SPGH and Cu(SPGH)2; (C) The CL spectra of luminol+H2O2, luminol+H2O2+Cu2+, and luminol+H2O2+Cu2++ SPGH, which obtained by using 2 mM luminol, 20 mM H2O2, 10 μM Cu2+ and 1 mM SPGH; (D) The ESR spectra of ⋅OH generated in detection systems; (E) The ESR spectra of 1O2 generated in detection systems.

(A) SPGH specific binding with Cu2+ to form a 2:1 SPGH-Cu2+ complex (Cu(SPGH)2) (a) and the procedure for peptide-assisted enhanced CRET (b), in which CL in 445 nm can efficiently induce the emission of QDs in 620 nm; (B) The CRET spectra generated by Cu2+ and Cu(SPGH)2.

Optimization the concentration of SPGH (A), the dilution ratio of COOH-ZnCdSe/ZnS QDs (B), the reaction time of SPGH and Cu2+ (C), and the concentration of the CL substrates: luminol (D), BIP (E) and H2O2 (F). Error bars represent standard deviations (n = 3).

(A) The CRET spectra and standard curve under varying concentrations of Cu2+, error bars represent standard deviations (n = 3); (B) the selectivity of this method towards common metal ions; (C) correlation analysis of the total serum Cu obtained by the CRET method and ICP-AES; (D) the levels of total serum Cu in healthy individuals and WD patients.
The recoveries and precision of Cu spiked in human serum obtained by CRET* (n = 6).

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The endogenous Cu2+ in the selected serum was 2.56 μM, as determined by ICP-AES.

head.ce:title: A specific short peptide-assisted enhanced chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CRET) for label-free and ratiometric detection of copper ions in complex samples

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Toxicology and Sanitary Chemistry

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Toxicology and Sanitary Chemistry, School of Public Health, Capital Medical University, No.10 Xitoutiao, You An Men, Beijing 100069, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A specific short peptide-assisted enhanced chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer system was constructed and applied to facile ratiometric detection of Cu2+ in serum

Convenient and rapid detection of Cu2+ in serum has important clinical significance for related diseases. Herein, a ratiometric detection of Cu2+ based on specific short peptide (SPGH)-assisted enhanced chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CRET) has been constructed. An interesting phenomenon that tetra-peptide with the sequence of Ser-Pro-Gly-His (SPGH) was able to specifically and remarkably enhance the catalytic performance of Cu2+ has been discovered, the enhancement mechanism is mainly due to the significant increasing generation of Cu2+-induced hydroxyl radical after addition of SPGH. The chemiluminescence (CL) of the luminol-H2O2 system was efficiently catalyzed by the formed Cu(SPGH)2 complex, which acts as an internal light source to triggers on the fluorescence of QDs via CRET, allowing the ratiometric detection of Cu2+ within 15 min with improved selectivity and sensitivity. The relative standard deviation was lower than 6.38 % due to the ratiometric signal improving its anti-interference ability. Importantly, it is feasible to detect total Cu in human serum, and the results were consistent with that of ICP-AES (r = 0.9321, n=55). The reliable, rapid, yet simple ratiometric detection of Cu2+ in serum based on SPGH-assisted enhanced CRET provides a promising potential alternative method for Cu2+ detection in clinical application.

ce:keyword.ce:text: inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry

ce:section.ce:para: Copper (Cu) is widely distributed in tissues and participates in cellular processes as coenzyme factors or the structural components of proteins [1]. Genes defects involved in Cu metabolism and Cu exposure are closely related to the occurrence of Wilson’s disease (WD), Parkinson's and Alzheimer’s diseases [2–4]. In practice, the determination of Cu2+ at trace levels is typically carried out by sophisticated instruments [5,6]. But they have not been popularized, especially in grass-roots healthcare centers. Therefore, great efforts have been taken to seek simple alternatives based on fluorescence, colorimetric, chemiluminescence (CL), electrochemical and other detection methods [7–12].

Among them, due to the needless of external excitation sources, the CL methods directly based on the catalytic activity of Cu2+ have drawn much attention. [13–17]. However, two obvious limitations exist for application to complex samples [18]. One of which is the insufficient selectivity due to the interference from Fe3+ above μM levels, and the other one is the low sensitivity caused by the inherent restricted catalytic capabilities of Cu2+. To deal with this, Cu2+-imprinted cross-linked chitosan resin, Cu-specific antibodies and several organic molecules were introduced to improve their selectivity or sensitivity [19–22], respectively. Nonetheless, simultaneously improving the sensitivity and specificity is still a challenge.

Additionally, single intensity-based CL assays are labile, and also usually suffered from oxidation of luminol by H2O2, and the generated CL leads to relatively high background signals. Contrarily, detection based on ratiometric signals could eliminate the majority of ambiguities by self-calibration of multiple emission bands [23]. In general, ratiometric detection occurs in two or more fluorophores with different emission wavelengths [24], however, ratiometric detection using CL as one emission has rarely been reported. Chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CRET), transfers energy from the CL system (donor) to excite the fluorescence of the acceptor [25,26], providing the possibility of ratiometric detection in CL systems. Besides, ratiometric detection based on CRET has an ideal low background due to the needless of eternal excitation light [27].

Interestingly, we found that specific short peptide (Ser-Pro-Gly-His, denoted as SPGH) combine with Cu2+ were able to dramatically improve the catalytic performance of Cu2+ in luminol-H2O2 CL system. Subsequently, the interaction between SPGH and Cu2+ and the reaction mechanism of SPGH-assisted enhanced catalytic performance of Cu2+ were systematically explored via isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, CL spectroscopy and electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy. After that, a novel peptide-assisted enhanced CRET for label-free and ratiometric detection of Cu2+ was constructed by using quantum dots (QDs) as desirable acceptors. It is worth noting that the SPGH used in this work could specifically recognize Cu2+ and increase the catalytic activity of Cu2+, thereby improving the detection sensitivity and specificity simultaneously.

Peptides were synthesized with purities of 98 % by Anhui Guoping Pharmaceutical Co. Ltd (China). Streptavidin-coated quantum dots (SA-ZnCdSe/ZnS QDs, 605 nm) and Carboxyl modified quantum dots (COOH-ZnCdSe/ZnS QDs, 625 nm) were purchased from Wuhan Jiayuan Quantum Dots Co. Ltd. (China). Luminol was bought from Sigma (USA). 4-phenylphenol (BIP) and KOH (EG, 99.999 %) were obtained from Aladdin. HNO3 (BV-III, 70 %) were obtained from Beijing Institute of Chemical Reagents (China). Standard solutions of metal ions (Cu2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Se2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Ca2+ and Mg2+) were purchased from National Standard Research Center (China).

Five peptides with different sequences were separately dissolved by 5 mM borate saline buffer (BS, pH 7.4) with a final concentration of 10 mM, the specific amino acid sequences were listed in Table S1. For screening of the peptide sequence, 25 μL of 10 mM peptides were incubated with 25 μL Cu2+ standard solutions of different concentrations for 30 min at room temperature, and the CL intensity was recorded immediately after auto-injection of CL substrate (luminol and H2O2). In addition, 25 μL 10 mM ligands (including SPGH, EDTA, Typ, Phe and the four amino acid composition in SPGH) were incubated with 25 μL 10 μM Cu2+ to investigate the enhancement capability of different ligands. Moreover, 10 mM SPGH (or EDTA) and 10 μM metal ions (Cu2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Se2+, Co2+, Ca2+ and Mg2+) were employed to study whether SPGH can specifically enhance the catalytic performance of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermodynamic parameters of the interaction between Cu2+ and SPGH
ce:section.ce:para: The thermodynamics of the binding between Cu2+ and SPGH were determined using Affinity ITC (TA Instruments, USA). 100 μM Cu2+ and 26.3 μM SPGH were prepared in 5 mM BS, pH 7.4. All solutions were degassed under vacuum for 10 min before titration. Titrations of Cu2+ into the buffer and SPGH were completed at 25 °C, with a stirring rate of 250 rpm. Enthalpy change (ΔH), entropy change (ΔS) and binding constants (Kd) for the interaction between Cu2+ and SPGH were obtained from ITC data using NanoAnalyze 3.8.0.0 software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The FTIR spectra of Cu2+ coordinated with SPGH
ce:section.ce:para: The changes of SPGH structure after Cu2+ coordination were studied by FTIR spectrometry. The concentration of SPGH and Cu2+ was 1.0 M and 5.0 mM, respectively. The FTIR spectra were recorded by Thermo Fisher Nicolet iS5 (iD5 ATR).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of SPGH-assisted enhanced the catalytic ability of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: First, the influence of Cu2+ and SPGH-Cu2+ complex on CL spectra was investigated to discuss whether there was any new emitter produced. Subsequently, the generation of free radicals during CL process were monitored by the ESR spectra (JEOL JES-FA300 ESR spectrometer), 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidine (TEMP) were used to specifically capture hydroxyl radical (⋅OH) and singlet oxygen (1O2), respectively. Briefly, 5 μL 0.05 M DMPO or TEMP added into the 20 μL SPGH-Cu2+-luminol-H2O2, the orders of samples are as follows: 5 μL 10 mM SPGH, 5 μL Cu2+ with different concentrations, 5 μL 0.05 M DMPO or TEMP, 5 μL 2 mM luminol, 5 μL 20 mM H2O2. The mixed reagent was immediately siphoned into the capillary tube for detection, the ESR spectra were recorded using the following parameters: microwave frequency, 9.4 GHz; microwave power, 10 mW; center field, 336 m T.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Peptide-assisted enhanced CRET for determination of Cu2
+
ce:section.ce:para: The peptide-assisted enhanced CRET for the determination of Cu2+ was performed in white 96-well plates. First, 25 μL of 10 mM SPGH was incubated with an equal volume of test samples (or Cu2+ standard solutions) for 15 min at room temperature. Then, 1 μL 10-fold diluted COOH-ZnCdSe/ZnS QDs (final concentration was 5.0 nM) was added. After that, the 96-well plates were into the SpectraMax i3x (Molecular Devices, USA), and the CL spectra were recorded immediately after auto-injection of CL substrate (luminol and H2O2) into the 96-well plates. The experimental parameters were optimized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ in complex samples
ce:section.ce:para: Serum from healthy individuals and WD patients were provided by Beijing Chao-yang Hospital, which were remaining samples for clinical testing. This experiment was approved by the Medical Ethics Committee of Capital Medical University (NO. 2014SY47) and performed in accordance with the relevant guidelines and regulations. A mixture (3 mL) of H2SO4, HNO3 and H2O2 (2:1:1) was added to 200 μL serum and heated at 150 °C until the samples were approximately dry. [28–30] The residues were dissolved in reaction buffer, and the pH was adjusted to about neutral using KOH. Finally, the digested samples were diluted and measured by the CRET method and ICP-AES, and the results were further analyzed by SPSS 19.0 software.

Interestingly, we found that short peptides binding with Cu2+ were able to dramatically improve the catalytic performance of Cu2+ in luminol-H2O2 CL system. In order to explore whether the enhancement eﬀ ;ect of peptides on Cu2+ catalyzed CL is sequence-dependent, five peptides with different sequences were screening. As shown in Fig. 1 A, peptides with the sequences of SPGH exhibited a particularly remarkable enhancement effect on catalytic ability of Cu2+. In addition, the enhancement capability of different ligands, including the four amino acid composition in SPGH (serine, proline, glycine and histidine), EDTA, tryptophan (Typ) and phenylalanine (Phe), has been studied. As displayed in Fig. 1B and Table S2, compared with EDTA, Typ, Phe and the four amino acids, SPGH, as a peptide, has the highest enhancement factor (regard as the ratio of CL in the presence of ligand to the CL in the presence of Cu2+ alone, CL(Ligand+Cu)/CL(Cu)) of luminol-H2O2 system. Moreover, whether SPGH can specifically enhance the catalytic performance of Cu2+ was investigated. As shown in Fig. 1C and Table S3, Chelates of EDTA-Fe3+ and EDTA-Co2+ could enhance the catalytic performance of Fe3+ and Co2+, which were consistent with the report. [19] Additionally, the CL generated by EDTA-Co2+ is 27-fold of EDTA-Cu2+. However, compared to EDTA, the CL generated by SPGH-Fe3+ and SPGH-Co2+ have no significant difference with that of Fe3+ and Co2+ itself, and the CL generated by SPGH-Cu2+ is 260-fold of SPGH-Co2+. Comprehensive consideration of the above results, SPGH could significantly and specifically enhance the catalytic performance of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermodynamic parameters of the interaction between Cu2+ and SPGH
ce:section.ce:para: ITC was employed to assess the interaction between SPGH and Cu2+. Fig. 2 A represents a typical thermogram of Cu2+ binding to SPGH at 25 °C. The corrected thermodynamic parameters of the interaction between Cu2+ and SPGH were calculated by the Bank (constant) model and Independent model, indicating that the binding stoichiometry between SPGH and Cu2+ is approximately 2:1, this result is consistent with molecular simulations in a previous report [31].

An intense interaction was found between Cu2+ and SPGH with a Kd of 6.502 × 10−7 M. In addition, the interaction of Cu2+ and SPGH was thermodynamically favorable as the ΔH and ΔS were -65.43 kJ/mol and -101.0 J/mol
K, respectively. The Gibbs’ energy change (ΔG) during the binding process was -35.32 kJ/mol, calculated from the equation ΔG=ΔH-TΔS. This finding suggests that the binding of the two is a spontaneous exothermic reaction, and the disorder of the system decreased after Cu2+ bound with SPGH. In general, a negative ΔS is frequently taken as evidence for van der Waals forces, and a negative ΔH is interpreted as evidence for hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions [32]. Since |ΔH| > |TΔS|, the binding of Cu2+ and SPGH was mainly driven by the ΔH of the reaction. That is, the binding reaction was primarily governed by hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions. These results indicated that SPGH enhanced the CRET by forming a stable complex with Cu2+ (Cu(SPGH)2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The FTIR spectra of SPGH and Cu(SPGH)2
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectra were employed to investigate the coordination between SPGH and Cu2+. The FTIR spectra of SPGH and Cu(SPGH)2 is displayed in Fig. 2B, typical peptide bands [33]: amide I (1700 ∼1600 cm−1), amide II (1600∼1500 cm−1) and amide III (1400∼1200 cm−1) can be observed. A band center at 1673 cm−1 can be found for free SPGH, and shifted to 1668 cm−1 for Cu(SPGH)2. This shift of band center indicates that Cu2+ may bind with either the O of the CO or the N of the CN of the backbone. Amide III is the region with the largest change in FTIR spectra after Cu2+ coordination. A combination peak of 1367 cm−1 and 1346 cm−1 can be observed in free SPGH, but the FTIR absorption peak at 1346 cm−1 was significantly enhanced in Cu(SPGH)2. This phenomenon demonstrated that the Cu2+ mainly coordinates with N on the histidine ring [34]. In summary, the coordination of Cu2+ with SPGH is mainly carried out by the N of the histidine ring and the O or N of the amide bond.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Explore the mechanism of SPGH-assisted enhanced catalytic capacity of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of SPGH enhanced the catalytic performance of Cu2+ have been explored. As shown in Fig. 2C, the maximum emission wavelength of CL spectra was still at 445 nm in the presence of Cu2+ or Cu(SPGH)2, it is consistent with that of luminol, indicating that the addition of Cu2+ or Cu(SPGH)2 did not lead to the formation of new illuminants, the emitter was still the oxidation of luminol, and the Cu2+ or Cu(SPGH)2 act as catalyst.

Previous researches have revealed that Cu2+ catalyze the H2O2-based CL system mainly due to the generation of hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH) [35,36]. Therefore, the free radicals in this system were measured by using JEOL JES-FA300 ESR spectrometer (X-band), DMPO and TEMP were used to specifically capture ⋅OH and singlet oxygen (1O2), respectively. As shown in Fig. 2D, compared with control groups and the Cu2+ group, significantly enhanced characteristic ESR spectrum of ⋅OH can be monitored after the addition of SPGH, and the ESR intensity of ⋅OH increases with the increasing concentration of Cu2+ (Fig. S1). However, the characteristic ESR spectrum of 1O2 has no significant change regardless of the presence or absence of Cu2+ and Cu(SPGH)2 (Fig. 2E). Therefore, the SPGH-assisted enhanced the catalytic ability of Cu2+ may mainly attributed to the significant increasing generation of Cu2+-induced ⋅OH after the addition of SPGH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The procedure and feasibility of peptide enhanced CRET
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the dramatic and specific enhancement effect of SPGH on Cu2+-catalyzed luminol-H2O2 CL system, a novel peptide-assisted enhanced CRET for label-free and ratiometric detection of Cu2+ was constructed. The molecular model of SPGH and the procedure for peptide-assisted enhanced CRET were illustrated in Fig. 3 A. SPGH formed a 2:1 complex with Cu2+ (denoted as Cu(SPGH)2). In the presence of QDs, the CL generated by the Cu(SPGH)2 complex acts as an internal light source (donor) to trigger the fluorescence of QDs (acceptor), resulting in CRET between luminol and the QDs. With increasing concentrations of Cu2+, the CL intensity of luminol emission (I 445) increased continuously, as did the CRET-stimulated fluorescence intensity of QDs emission (I 620). The luminescence intensity ratios (R=I 445/I 620) positively correlated with the concentration of Cu2+, allowing for the ratiometric detection of Cu2+ based on the peptide-assisted enhanced CRET.

The feasibility of CRET was verified and displayed in Fig. 3B, the CL system was catalysed by 10 μM or 20 μM Cu2+ to emit light at 445 nm through the decomposition of H2O2, but the emission of COOH-ZnCdSe/ZnS QDs at 620 nm was not observed. Interestingly, upon addition 5 mM SPGH to 1 μM Cu2+, the weak CL signal at 445 nm was significantly enhanced, and induced the CRET process, evident by the intense fluorescence emission of COOH-ZnCdSe/ZnS QDs at 620 nm. Control experiments indicated that no CRET-induced fluorescence of QDs was detected in the presence of SPGH alone. During the CRET process, positively charged Cu(SPGH)2 complex combined with negatively charged COOH-ZnCdSe/ZnS QDs through electrostatic interaction was confirmed by DLS (Fig. S2). Furtherly, Cu(SPGH)2 served as the catalytic center induce more luminol to generate CL (donors) around the COOH-ZnCdSe/ZnS QDs, thereby induced efficient CRET by provides sufficient CL intensity while guarantee the distance between energy donors and acceptors. Additionally, the CRET efficiency (E) was calculated to be 12.39 % according to the following equation: E=I A/(I A+I D) ×100 %, where I A and I D correspond to the integral area of the acceptor and donor emissions, respectively. Since the Förster distance of luminol-H2O2-QDs CRET system was in the range of 3–3.5 nm,[26] the distance between the acceptor and donor was calculated in the range of 4.2–4.8 nm.

The experimental parameters were optimized in the presence of 2 μM Cu2+. Label-free SPGH, COOH-ZnCdSe/ZnS QDs and 5 mM BS (pH 7.4) were used for the CRET (Fig. S3-S5). As shown in Fig. 4 A, I 620 increased to a maximum at 10 mM SPGH, but decreased with further addition of SPGH due to the reduction in CRET efficiency caused by excessive peptides. Therefore, 10 mM SPGH was selected as the optimal dose. Additionally, the concentration of the acceptor (COOH-ZnCdSe/ZnS QDs) was studied (Fig. 4B). I 445 decreased continuously with increasing concentration of COOH-ZnCdSe/ZnS QDs, while the I 620 increased, showing that the acceptor concentration also affects the CRET efficiency, this result was consistent with previous reports [37,38]. That is, in a particular CRET system, a high concentration of the acceptor results in a highly efficient of CRET. In this study, a 10-fold dilution of COOH-ZnCdSe/ZnS QDs with the final concentration of 5.0 nM was chosen by considering the balance between cost and CRET efficiency. Besides, the peptide-assisted enhanced CRET directly relied on the yield of the Cu(SPGH)2 complex, which, in turn corresponded to the incubation time of SPGH and Cu2+. Fig. 4C exhibits that only 15 min was needed for the interaction between SPGH and Cu2+ to reach equilibrium. Furthermore, the concentrations of CL substrates, including luminol, BIP (used as the luminescence enhancer of luminol-H2O2 system, which can significantly enhance the chemiluminescence intensity of and prolong the lifetime of chemiluminescence) and H2O2, were optimized to achieve a more efficient CRET. Accordingly, 2 mM luminol, 0.04 mM BIP and 20 mM H2O2 were selected for subsequent investigations (Fig. 4D–F).

The peptide-assisted enhanced CRET developed here was applied to detect Cu2+ under the optimized conditions. Fig. 5 A depicts the luminescence spectra of the system in the presence of various concentrations of Cu2+. The I 445 increased as the concentration of Cu2+ increased, as well as the I 620. Moreover, the logarithmic value of the I 445/I 620 showed a good linear relationship with the logarithmic value of the Cu2+ concentration in the range of 200 nM∼10 μM (inset in Fig. 5A), and an R2 value of 0.9864 was achieved. Additionally, the limit of detection (LOD) of this method was obtained by experiment. 10 blank samples were detected to obtain
X ¯
0+3SD, where
X ¯
0 and SD were the average value and the standard deviation of I 445/I 620 for 10 blank samples, respectively. The LOD was regarded as the Cu2+ concentration corresponding to I 445/I 620 >
X ¯
0+3SD. Therefore, the LOD of this peptide-assisted enhanced CRET method was 50 nM, which is 20 times lower than the LOD obtained by directly catalysing CL with Cu2+ under the same conditions (Fig. S6).

10 common metal ions with concentrations of 100 μM were used to investigate the selectivity of this method. As displayed in Fig. 5B, the spectra for Mn2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Se2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ni2+ and Cr3+ were not different from that given by buffer. Although Fe3+ and Co2+ are considered to be the largest interfering factor in the Cu2+ catalytic CL reaction (Fig. S7-S8) [39], the intensity of CL generated by Fe3+ and Co2+ were not enough to induce a CRET. However, the spectrum corresponding to 1 μM Cu2+ showed an efficient CRET, due to SPGH specifically binding to Cu2+ and significantly enhancing the Cu2+ catalysed CL reaction. Consequently, the effect of common metal ions coexisting in complex samples on Cu2+ detection can be effectively eliminated through CRET method.

Human serum with an endogenous Cu concentration of 2.56 μM, as determined by ICP-AES, was spiked with Cu2+ standard solutions to evaluate the accuracy and precision of this method. These spiked samples were digested and then diluted 5-fold and tested 6 times to determine the recovery of Cu2+ from human serum. Additionally, replicated measurements were performed in the same day and on 6 different days to obtain intra- and inter-assays precision. The results are displayed in Table 1 , the intra- and inter- assay recoveries of Cu2+ were 92.76 % ∼ 104.70 % and 98.89 % ∼ 109.86 %, respectively, with corresponding relative standard deviations (RSD) of 2.88 % ∼ 5.91 % and 3.82 % ∼ 6.38 %, respectively. The recovery rate and precision of CRET are both more desirable than SPGH-assisted enhanced CL (Fig. S7). These results demonstrate that the ratiometric signal from CRET could eliminate the interference form optical path length, probe concentration, et al., thus ensuring the accuracy and stability of the detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cu2+ in complex samples
ce:section.ce:para: Serum is one of the most representative complex samples. Cu in serum is an essential indicator for diagnosis and follow-up examination of WD, which is a typical Cu metabolism disorder prone to occur in adolescents, with a worldwide prevalence of 1/10,000 to 1/30,000 [40]. Therefore, to validate the feasibility of this proposed method, serum from WD patients and healthy individuals was analysed by this method and ICP-AES. A good correlation was obtained between the two methods with a correlation coefficient of 0.9321 (n = 55) (Fig. 5C). Furthermore, paired sample t-tests showed the differences between the two methods had no statistical significance (P > 0.01). This result demonstrated that the peptide-assisted enhanced CRET has tremendous potential for effective detection of Cu2+ in complex samples with high accuracy. Additionally, the reference ranges of total serum Cu for healthy individuals and WD patients were 16.06 ± 2.99 μM (n = 35) and 5.28 ± 2.93 μM (n = 20), respectively (Fig. 5D). Significant decrease in total serum Cu was found in WD patients (P < 0.01) by using independent sample t-test statistical analysis, which was consistent with clinical features of WD [40].

In summary, an interesting phenomenon that peptide with the sequence of SPGH was able to specifically and remarkably enhance the catalytic performance of Cu2+ has been discovered. Through an enthalpy change dominated interaction between SPGH and Cu2+, a 2:1 SPGH-Cu2+ complex (Cu(SPGH)2) was formed, with a Kd of 6.502 × 10−7 M. This Cu(SPGH)2 complex enhance the catalytic performance of Cu2+ may be mainly attributed to the significant increasing generation of Cu-induced ⋅OH after the addition of SPGH. Subsequently, a novel label-free and ratiometric detection of Cu2+, based on SPGH-assisted enhanced CRET, has been developed by utilizing the low background of CRET and self-calibrated ratiometric signal. This approach exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity (LOD = 50 nM), good reproducibility and stability with a RSD lower than 6.38 %. The correlation coefficient between this proposed CRET method and ICP-AES for the detection of Cu2+ in human serum was 0.9321, highlighting its applicability for clinical testing. Moreover, this procedure can be performed in a 96-well plate within 15 min with very low cost owing to not requiring expensive instruments or modified reagents. To conclude, this novel CRET-based strategy was rapid, cost-effective, yet simple, and expected to be further developed into an easily conducted diagnostic kit.

Yamin Xiong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Lihong Zhou: Formal analysis, Visualization. Xiaoxia Peng: Investigation, Validation. Huiling Li: Formal analysis, Resources. Hui Wang: Validation. Leiliang He: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Peili Huang: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81573201, 21605131) and the Beijing Natural Science Foundation Program and Scientific Research Key Program of Beijing Municipal Commission of Education (KZ201510025027).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128411.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Redox- and non-redox-metal-induced formation of free radicals and their role in human disease

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Valko, K. Jomova, C.J. Rhodes, K. Kuča, K. Musílek, Redox- and non-redox-metal-induced formation of free radicals and their role in human disease, Arch. Toxicol. 90 (2016) 1-37.
A. Ala, A.P. Walker, K. Ashkan, J.S. Dooley, M.L. Schilsky, Wilson's disease, Lancet, 369 (2007) 397-408.

P. Ferenci, A. Członkowska, U. Merle, S. Ferenc, G. Gromadzka, C. Yurdaydin, et al., Late-Onset Wilson’s Disease, Gastroenterology, 132 (2007) 1294-1298.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yamin Xiong is a Ph.D at Department of Toxicology and Sanitary Chemistry, School of Public Health, Capital Medical University, China. Her research interests focus on developing nanoprobes for bioanalysis.
Lihong Zhou is currently a master candidate at Department of Toxicology and Sanitary Chemistry, School of Public Health, Capital Medical University, China.
Xiaoxia Peng is currently a director of the center for clinical epidemiology and evidence-based medicine, Beijing Children's Hospital, Capital Medical University, China.
Huiling Li is currently an associate senior technician at Department of Occupational Medicine and Clinical Toxicology, Beijing Chaoyang Hospital, Capital Medical University, China.
Hui Wang is currently an associate professor at Department of Toxicology and Sanitary Chemistry, School of Public Health, Capital Medical University, China.

Leiliang He is currently an associate professor at College of Public Health, Zhengzhou University, China. His research interests focus on the design and development of nanoprobe for detection and bioanalysis.

Peili Huang is currently a professor at Department of Toxicology and Sanitary Chemistry, School of Public Health, Capital Medical University, China. Her research interests focus on rapid detection technology.
